refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature database) and can be queried by downstream planning and execution functions to retrieve spatial attributes (position, geometry, classification) for an agreed set of site features.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and restricted areas in the spatial model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and provide access to the worksite spatial model for planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign zone-level feature inventories (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; maintain zone-feature mappings in a relational schema; provide zone-based query API.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a voxel grid with occupancy and semantic labels; update grid incrementally as new observations arrive; convert grid to feature primitives (planes, edges, clusters) on demand for planning queries.

DP4: Acquire raw sensor streams (camera, lidar, IMU) and store as time-indexed data lake; defer feature extraction and representation to query time; provide lazy evaluation of spatial models on consumer request.

DP5: Integrate worksite safety plan and operational policies as constraints into the spatial model; represent features with associated policy rules (e.g., exclusion zones, speed limits, access permissions); expose constrained feature set to planning/execution via policy-aware query interface.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP5 (integrated): Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; represent as a structured spatial graph with feature geometry, classification, and safety/policy attributes; maintain bidirectional traceability between raw observations and derived features; expose via a policy-aware query interface that filters and annotates features according to worksite safety plan and operational policies.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor fusion logic and spatial representation format (mitigated by abstracting fusion as a pluggable module with standardized output contract).
Dependency on policy schema definition; if policies change frequently, representation must remain flexible (mitigated by storing policies separately and applying as overlay/filter).
Coordinate frame alignment across multi-modal sensors introduces calibration coupling (mitigated by centralizing calibration as a distinct DP and versioning it with the model).

Query interface design must remain agnostic to downstream planner/executor specifics to avoid over-specification (mitigated by defining queries in terms of feature predicates, not task-specific semantics).
coupling_check.notes: DP1+DP5 avoids the premature commitment of DP2 (zone decomposition) and DP4 (lazy evaluation), both of which defer critical decisions or introduce latency/complexity. DP3 (voxel grid) is rejected because it obscures semantic feature boundaries and complicates policy annotation. DP1+DP5 balances early feature extraction (enabling timely planning) with policy flexibility (supporting safety/operational constraints).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Multi-modal sensor suite (vision, range, inertial) is available and calibrated; RISK: If sensors are unreliable or poorly calibrated, fusion quality degrades and downstream planning fails. MITIGATION: Establish sensor health monitoring and calibration verification as part of model initialization.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are well-defined and stable during a planning/execution cycle; RISK: If policies change mid-cycle, model must be re-queried or invalidated. MITIGATION: Version policies and tag model snapshots with policy version; define policy change notification protocol.

ASSUMPTION: Spatial features can be meaningfully classified and represented in a unified coordinate frame; RISK: Dynamic or ambiguous features (e.g., temporary obstacles, personnel) may not fit static model. MITIGATION: Distinguish static (infrastructure) from dynamic (transient) features; refresh dynamic features at appropriate intervals.

ASSUMPTION: Query interface is expressive enough to support diverse planning/execution strategies; RISK: Unforeseen query patterns may require model restructuring. MITIGATION: Design query interface around feature predicates (geometry, classification, policy tags) rather than task-specific semantics; support extensible attribute schema.

ASSUMPTION: Acceptance criteria ('agreed set of site features') implies stakeholder consensus on feature taxonomy; RISK: Disagreement on what constitutes a 'feature' or how to classify it. MITIGATION: Establish feature taxonomy as a separate artifact; validate against safety plan and operational policies before model deployment.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) the set of feature types to be captured (e.g., static obstacles, hazard zones, access points, equipment locations, personnel exclusion areas); (2) the required spatial accuracy and update frequency; (3) the policy attributes that must be attached to features (e.g., safety constraints, access rules, speed limits); (4) the query patterns expected from planning/execution functions (e.g., 'find all obstacles within 2m of path', 'list access points for zone X', 'check if location is in exclusion zone'). This will sharpen the DP specification and enable clearer IM boundaries (e.g., sensor fusion module, spatial graph store, policy query engine).

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for acquiring multi-modal spatial feature data, fusing observations into a unified coordinate frame, maintaining a structured spatial graph with feature geometry/classification/safety attributes, managing bidirectional traceability, and exposing a policy-aware query interface filtered by worksite safety plans and operational policies.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw multi-modal sensor observations (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; feeds spatial feature acquisition pipeline.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits policy-filtered safety annotations and feature-derived alerts based on spatial graph queries and worksite safety plan constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational policy directives and safety plan updates; returns policy-aware feature query results to guide task execution and safety validation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits spatial graph state and traceability metadata to remote operators; receives policy updates and safety plan revisions from command center.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Safety Services is the natural owner of spatial feature fusion, safety attribute management, policy-aware filtering, and traceability. Core DP logic (multi-modal fusion, spatial graph, safety/policy attributes, query interface) is cohesive within safety domain. Minor diffusion: raw sensor acquisition (Telemetry in) and task guidance (Tasking out) cross boundaries, but these are well-defined interface contracts. Bidirectional traceability is internal to the module.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a platform/hardware abstraction layer. Spatial feature fusion, graph representation, and policy-aware query logic are not vehicle-specific concerns. Placing DP here would scatter safety-critical reasoning across vehicle control logic, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion: safety attributes, policy filtering, and traceability would leak into vehicle subsystems.

Weak fit: Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command relay layer. While it may consume spatial feature queries for operator visualization, it should not own the fusion, graph, or policy-aware filtering logic. Placing DP here conflates operator interaction with safety-critical spatial reasoning. High diffusion: core fusion and traceability logic would need to be replicated or exposed across multiple subsystems.

Very poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container/orchestrator. It should not directly own detailed spatial feature fusion and policy-aware query logic; this is too low-level and domain-specific for a solution-level module. Placing DP here would bloat the orchestrator and obscure safety-critical responsibilities. Extreme diffusion: fusion, graph, and policy logic would be scattered across subsystems.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most cohesive and least diffuse boundary for DP1 + DP5 because: (1) spatial feature fusion with safety/policy attributes is fundamentally a safety-domain concern; (2) bidirectional traceability between raw observations and derived features is internal to the safety reasoning pipeline; (3) policy-aware query filtering directly implements safety plan constraints, which is the core responsibility of Safety Services; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert/Tasking/Network Link out) are clean and semantically aligned with safety service responsibilities; (5) no other module has both the domain expertise and architectural position to own this logic without creating tight coupling or responsibility diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: Telemetry interface may deliver high-volume raw sensor streams; Safety Services must implement efficient buffering and streaming fusion to avoid bottleneck. Mitigation: design fusion pipeline with asynchronous, batched processing and backpressure handling.

Risk: Policy and safety plan updates (via Tasking/Network Link) may arrive during active spatial graph queries; concurrent access to graph structure could cause inconsistency. Mitigation: implement versioned graph snapshots and atomic policy update transactions.

Risk: Bidirectional traceability (raw observation → derived feature) may create large metadata overhead if not carefully indexed. Mitigation: use sparse indexing and lazy materialization of trace links.

Risk: Policy-aware query interface may become a performance bottleneck if Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems poll it at high frequency. Mitigation: implement subscription-based alert model (SafetyAlert) rather than pull-based queries for time-critical safety decisions.

Risk: Spatial graph representation and fusion algorithm are complex; if not well-documented and modularized internally, Safety Services may become a monolithic black box. Mitigation: decompose Safety Services internally into sub-modules (Sensor Fusion, Spatial Graph, Policy Engine, Query Interface) with clear internal contracts.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. DP1 + DP5 is well-suited to Safety Services as stated. However, to further improve containment and reduce internal complexity, consider explicitly decomposing the DP into internal sub-DPs: (1) DP1a: Acquire and buffer multi-modal sensor streams (Telemetry interface); (2) DP1b: Fuse buffered observations into unified coordinate frame (internal); (3) DP5a: Represent fused features as structured spatial graph with geometry and classification (internal); (4) DP5b: Annotate graph features with safety/policy attributes (internal, driven by Tasking/Network Link); (5) DP5c: Expose policy-aware query interface with traceability (SafetyAlert/Tasking/Network Link interfaces). This internal decomposition keeps the IM boundary (Safety Services) intact while clarifying the internal module structure and reducing perceived monolithicity.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') maps directly to the IM boundary (Safety Services) with clear, measurable acceptance criteria: (1) worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions; (2) agreed set of site features is captured; (3) model is machine-readable and queryable. Safety Services' external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert/Tasking/Network Link out) provide observable contract points for verification. Traceability from raw observations to derived features is auditable within the module.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. Safety Services is unambiguously the owner of spatial feature capture, fusion, representation, and exposure. The IM boundary is semantically aligned with the FR: spatial feature modeling is a safety-domain concern, not a vehicle control or teleoperation concern. No ambiguity about which module is responsible for generating the worksite model or maintaining feature traceability.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 + DP5 (integrated) is a logically sound and complete solution to the FR. DP1 (multi-modal sensing + fusion into unified coordinate frame) directly addresses 'capture' of spatial features. DP5 (structured spatial graph with geometry, classification, safety/policy attributes + policy-aware query interface) directly addresses 'represent in machine-readable model' and 'available to planning/execution functions.' Bidirectional traceability ensures auditability and safety compliance. The DP is neither over-specified nor under-specified; it provides sufficient detail to guide IM design without prescribing implementation technology. Integration of DP1 and DP5 is justified: fusion and representation are tightly coupled in the spatial reasoning pipeline.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry interface high-volume streaming: Safety Services must implement efficient buffering, batching, and backpressure handling to avoid bottleneck. Mitigation: asynchronous fusion pipeline with configurable batch windows and queue depth limits.

Concurrent access to spatial graph during policy updates: Policy/safety plan changes (via Tasking/Network Link) may race with active feature queries. Mitigation: implement versioned graph snapshots and atomic policy update transactions with read-write lock or MVCC semantics.

Bidirectional traceability metadata overhead: Maintaining raw observation → derived feature links may create large indexing and storage costs. Mitigation: use sparse indexing, lazy materialization, and configurable retention policies for trace links.

Policy-aware query interface polling bottleneck: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems may poll Safety Services at high frequency for feature queries, creating latency and resource contention. Mitigation: prefer subscription-based SafetyAlert model for time-critical safety decisions; reserve pull-based queries for planning-time operations.

Internal monolithicity risk: Spatial graph representation, fusion algorithm, and policy engine are complex; without clear internal sub-module boundaries, Safety Services may become opaque and difficult to test/maintain. Mitigation: decompose internally into Sensor Fusion, Spatial Graph, Policy Engine, and Query Interface sub-modules with documented internal contracts.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Containment score of 0.92 for Safety Services is well-justified. The DP is cohesive within the safety domain: multi-modal fusion, spatial graph representation, safety/policy attribute management, and policy-aware filtering are all core safety-reasoning concerns. External interfaces are clean and semantically aligned: Telemetry (raw sensor in) and SafetyAlert/Tasking/Network Link (policy-filtered features out) are well-defined contract points. No other module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) has the domain expertise or architectural position to own this logic without creating tight coupling or responsibility diffusion. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and reflects necessary cross-boundary data flow (sensor input, task guidance, policy updates), not logic scatter. Recommended internal decomposition into sub-DPs (DP1a–DP5c) will further reduce perceived monolithicity while keeping the IM boundary intact.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly decompose Safety Services internally into five sub-modules with clear internal contracts: (1) Sensor Fusion (DP1a/DP1b): acquire, buffer, and fuse multi-modal observations; (2) Spatial Graph (DP5a): represent fused features as structured graph with geometry and classification; (3) Policy Engine (DP5b): annotate graph features with safety/policy attributes and manage policy updates; (4) Query Interface (DP5c): expose policy-aware feature queries with traceability; (5) Traceability Manager (internal): maintain bidirectional links between raw observations and derived features. This internal structure reduces monolithicity and clarifies ownership of sub-concerns.

Design Telemetry interface with explicit backpressure and buffering contract: specify maximum queue depth, batch window, and overflow behavior (drop, block, or sample). Implement asynchronous fusion pipeline with configurable throughput limits to prevent bottleneck.

Implement versioned spatial graph snapshots and atomic policy update transactions: use read-write locks or MVCC to ensure consistency during concurrent queries and policy updates. Document transaction semantics and isolation levels.

Establish sparse indexing and lazy materialization strategy for bidirectional traceability: define retention policies for trace links (e.g., keep only recent N observations per feature, or prune after T seconds). Measure metadata overhead and optimize as needed.

Prefer subscription-based SafetyAlert model over pull-based queries for time-critical safety decisions: Vehicle and Teleoperation should subscribe to relevant safety alerts rather than polling Safety Services. Reserve pull-based queries for planning-time operations with relaxed latency requirements.

Document internal sub-module interfaces and data contracts: specify message formats, latency budgets, and error handling for Sensor Fusion → Spatial Graph → Policy Engine → Query Interface pipeline. Include examples of policy update propagation and traceability query execution.

Add acceptance test for worksite model availability: verify that planning/execution functions (e.g., Tasking subsystem) can successfully query Safety Services for an agreed set of site features within specified latency bounds. Include test cases for policy-filtered feature queries and traceability retrieval.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence and excellent allocation clarity. The FR is well-scoped, the DP is logically sound and complete, and the IM boundary (Safety Services) is unambiguous and semantically aligned. However, the recommended_revisions explicitly call for internal decomposition of Safety Services into five sub-modules (Sensor Fusion, Spatial Graph, Policy Engine, Query Interface, Traceability Manager) to reduce monolithicity and clarify ownership of sub-concerns. This is a strong signal to derive next-level sub-FRs that map to these internal sub-modules. The diffusion_risks (particularly 'Internal monolithicity risk') and the high safety criticality of the system justify deeper decomposition to ensure testability, maintainability, and auditability of the safety-critical spatial reasoning pipeline. Proceeding to next-level decomposition will operationalize the recommended internal structure and establish clear contracts between sub-modules.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Acquire, buffer, and fuse multi-modal sensor observations (raw telemetry) into a unified coordinate frame with configurable batch windows and backpressure handling.
FR1.2: Represent fused spatial features as a structured graph with geometry, classification, and semantic attributes queryable by downstream planning/execution functions.
FR1.3: Annotate spatial graph features with safety/policy attributes and manage atomic updates to safety policies without race conditions during concurrent feature queries.
FR1.4: Expose policy-aware feature queries to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems with traceability links to raw observations and specified latency bounds for planning-time operations.
FR1.5: Maintain bidirectional traceability between raw sensor observations and derived spatial features with configurable retention policies and sparse indexing to manage metadata overhead.

revise.why: The current DP (DP1 + DP5 integrated) is logically sound but describes a monolithic solution. To operationalize the recommended internal decomposition and reduce the 0.08 diffusion risk, explicitly decompose the DP into five sub-DPs that map to the proposed internal sub-modules: DP1a (Sensor Acquisition & Buffering), DP1b (Multi-Modal Fusion), DP5a (Spatial Graph Representation), DP5b (Policy Engine & Attribute Management), DP5c (Query Interface & Traceability). This decomposition will clarify internal contracts, enable independent testing of sub-concerns, and provide a clear roadmap for IM design of Safety Services' internal architecture. Include explicit interface specifications (message formats, latency budgets, error handling) between sub-DPs.

assumptions: Safety Services can be internally decomposed into five cohesive sub-modules without violating the IM boundary or creating external diffusion.
Sensor Fusion (DP1a/DP1b) can be decoupled from Spatial Graph (DP5a) via a well-defined feature stream interface with configurable batching and backpressure.
Policy Engine (DP5b) can manage atomic updates to safety attributes without blocking concurrent feature queries via versioned snapshots or MVCC semantics.
Query Interface (DP5c) can expose both subscription-based (SafetyAlert) and pull-based (planning-time) query modes with different latency/throughput characteristics.
Traceability Manager can maintain bidirectional observation-to-feature links with sparse indexing and lazy materialization without exceeding acceptable metadata overhead.
Internal sub-module interfaces can be documented and tested independently before integration into the full Safety Services module.
open_risks: Telemetry streaming bottleneck: Sensor Fusion must handle high-volume, variable-rate telemetry without dropping observations or blocking upstream Vehicle subsystem. Backpressure contract and queue depth limits must be validated under peak load.

Policy update race conditions: Concurrent policy updates (via Tasking/Network Link) and feature queries (via SafetyAlert/pull) may create consistency violations. MVCC or read-write lock semantics must be formally specified and tested.

Traceability metadata explosion: Sparse indexing and lazy materialization strategies must be validated to ensure metadata overhead remains acceptable (< 10% of feature storage, estimated). Retention policies must be tunable and monitored.

Query latency SLA: Planning-time pull-based queries must meet specified latency bounds (e.g., < 100ms p95) under concurrent load. Subscription-based SafetyAlert model must have lower latency (e.g., < 50ms p95) for time-critical decisions.

Internal sub-module coupling: Spatial Graph, Policy Engine, and Query Interface are tightly coupled; changes to graph schema or policy representation may require coordinated updates across sub-modules. Internal versioning and deprecation strategies must be established.

Testability of fusion algorithm: Multi-modal sensor fusion is complex and safety-critical; unit testing with synthetic sensor data may not capture real-world sensor noise, timing skew, or failure modes. Integration testing with real or high-fidelity simulated telemetry is essential.
evidence_needed: Detailed internal sub-module interface specifications (DP1a → DP1b → DP5a → DP5b → DP5c) with message formats, latency budgets, error handling, and backpressure semantics.
Telemetry streaming load profile and peak throughput estimates (observations/sec, bytes/sec) to validate Sensor Fusion buffering and batching strategy.
Policy update frequency and concurrency model (e.g., how often are safety policies updated? how many concurrent queries during an update?) to inform MVCC/locking design.

Traceability query patterns and retention requirements (e.g., how long must observation-to-feature links be retained? what is the typical query latency requirement?) to validate sparse indexing strategy.
Latency SLA for planning-time queries and time-critical SafetyAlert notifications (e.g., p50, p95, p99 latency targets) to guide Query Interface design and load testing.
Sensor fusion algorithm validation: test results on real or high-fidelity simulated multi-modal telemetry (camera, lidar, radar, IMU) showing accuracy, latency, and robustness to sensor failures.
Internal sub-module integration test plan: specify test cases for policy update propagation, concurrent query handling, traceability retrieval, and backpressure scenarios.

Acceptance test for worksite model availability: verify that Tasking subsystem can query Safety Services for agreed site features within specified latency bounds, with policy-filtered results and traceability links.
